steps.title: Identify the key contrast in how the passage treats each figure
steps.content:
Wladyslaw I Herman is described as “well-documented” and presented straightforwardly.

Lestek, by contrast, is introduced with the phrase “whose historical actuality is disputed,” signaling that historians disagree about whether he actually existed.

This is the decisive clue.

steps.title: Match the contrast to the correct choice
steps.content:
Choice B states that historians agree Wladyslaw I Herman existed but disagree about Lestek's existence—this directly reflects the passage's contrast.

The passage doesn't claim the Gesta provides more evidence for Lestek (A rules itself out); it says Lestek's documentation relies on oral tradition unlike later rulers, meaning less reliable evidence.

Choice C reverses the timeline—Lestek ruled in the 9th–10th centuries, not later than Wladyslaw (1079–1102).

Choice D incorrectly treats both figures as supported by “similar stories,” when the passage distinguishes Lestek's oral tradition from Wladyslaw's documented records.

That matches choice B — B is correct.
